item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image originates from here, a Louise Bourgeois artwork. As there is no indication this image was ever released under CC 3.0, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a really nitpicky complaint:

If the individual siblings are assigned alphabetical codes starting from SCP-1791-A, then it is terrible cataloging to refer to them collectively as SCP-1791-X. X is a letter! That would be poor form even there was a known, fixed number of objects, but evidence suggests a couple of these people pop up every few decades or so meaning it gets even more awkward as time goes on.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: Don't see what you're apparently seeing, so it looks like someone fixed that for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Um, I tried to fix some formatting, and it seems… stuck at how it is. What's that about? Why is the interview so screwed up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
unique, well written, scary without being outright horrifying

the only thing I have to criticize is the lack of more interviews with other instances of 1791-X
(though I don't know how it could add anything to the overall story, I was just sad it was already over)

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quite neat.

Also you have two of "and" at the end of the larger paragraph before the first addendum.

Take your +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of those 'how the hell did the author think of it?' ones. I dig it a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This deserves more attention.

item.title: Re: Photo at top
item.content:encoded: Trumpy, you are magic!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Brilliant! +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My bad, not sure how I missed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yo, could somebody tag this thing for me, since I don't know what the fuck I'm doing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beautiful. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Entries -C/-D and -H/-J would imply that the rest of them are by distinct fathers. Which makes for 7 baby-daddies; Omega seems to be …quite popular.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wonderfully done. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'mnot sure how this article makes me feel. There's a lot more depth here than I'm used to!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is in my top-ten SCPs. Congratulations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I also thought the hermit-crab idea was intentional.

This has a mixed emotional impact. On the one hand, it feels heavy, like you can't get away from where you've been. Yet there is a large feeling of comfort. Just broke up with your girl? Run back to a previous residence for awhile. Let the real world just go away. There's a security to knowing all that you've ever cared about is never going to be lost. Feeds into the basis for hoarding, not wanting to lose things.

Add to that the adoption issue. Children react to adoption in varying ways, depending on how old they are and how it's handled. But the biggest issue shared by most adopted children (excepting those that don't know) is a feeling of abandonment. Building copies of everywhere you were happy or spent any real time in is the total security blanket. It won't leave you, ever.

Oh and Omega has me soooo curious…'cause that's a LOT of kids. Have we screened for number of fathers?

+1 Great first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My brain wasn't going in quite that literal a direction, but you're completely right. They're goddamn hermit crabs; always dragging their shell around with them. That's incredibly brilliant and I wish I'd thought of it. If I could upboat your post, I would.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought it was sort of intentional. The spiral reminds me of a shell, after all…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: HOW DID I NOT SEE THAT

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They're human hermit crabs!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you, and yes. She's got a couple more decades before the Foundation starts seriously worrying, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A nice blend of bizarre and metaphoric, with a good execution holding everything together.

Also, my god, -E was born in 1910? Is she still alive?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Real nice feel to the this one.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep. astronautilus = "star-sailor". But it makes people think of space. So.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Guilty. Such a sucker for logarithmic spirals. Ugh math why must you make me love you

I'm glad you get the place I wrote this from; it's supposed to be a piece that touches on some serious emotional territory, but from odd angles. What would you do if you couldn't ever lose something important to you, or forget your past, or start things over? I think after a while it would make you even lonlier than you might ever have been.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Astro = "star", not "space"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's like a space in the shape of a nautilus shell… space… nautilus. Could it be? An astro…nautilus!?

In other news, this is nicely heartwarming/adorable, sad, and really crazy at the same time. It reads to me like you've took a good emotional theme, and built all of the weirdness as a frame for the theme. Reminds me a little of SCP-1782, and it manages to circumvent the main gripe I have about some of the really 'out there' article, since it doesn't for a moment feel like you're being bizarre for the sake of being bizarre.

+1, gold star and everything. You've done a really great job here, and good job for getting help with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I just cut that because it was kind of cheap.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really, really enjoy this, especially given that it's a first. I admit, I think it'd be cool as heck to have one of these. I mean, maybe minus the uterus section, but hey.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty outstanding. I have to agree that the SCP designations are a little confusing, but that is my only, minor complaint. Great work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is wonderful work for a first shot. There are a few oddities to the structure, and the component designations get a little awkward, but it works. The only thing that really throws me is the transfer request at the end of the interview log.

In any case, welcome to the site sir. I look forward to what else we may see from you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty much the first thing in a while that's really unsettled me.

Congrats on the first article. Look forward to the next one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you (I am the brokenest broken record of thanking everyone for everything). When I wrote this, I was thinking about what a lot of military and otherwise compulsarily nomadic parents want for their kids: a nice, stable, safe place to call home, which is of course the opposite of what a lot of military brats are getting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've always believed in me, azzleflux

(for real though thank you)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ^^I make better. And wow, that's a pretty great thing to say around here. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's like a person Nautilus shell… This is the most bizarre thing I've seen on the site for a while.

I'ma give you my +1, but NOTHING TO SEE HERE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well this is super duper weird. The idea of being unable to escape your home and past is kind of interesting, and the interview log and weirdly similar pasts of all the instances are a nice touch. Whole-hearted +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is my first SCP. A lot of folks helped me and I'm very grateful to them.
